
= Core philosophy =
The main driver behind this is making LLM assisted development verifiable.
It is essentially the same ting we've always done, but structured in a way that should make
followup-work, fixes and verification more structured, making it easier for humans to get an overview,
and for LLM agents to work with.

I feel this should be something like "git for LLM development". It should be a tool where each step
could be executed by a human or a machine, and noted as such.
Outputs and intermediate results should be possible to diff and review with clear indication of
author (human or machine). Something like git blame, where the path leads back towards implementation discussions and initial specification.

It is also a way of controlling context going into each step. For example, implementation should maybe not have
access to any other context than the planned implementation steps and the files that were identified as sources or targets by the planning stage.


- Iplementation work can also produce information nodes (documentation, for example):
  - Other nodes can depend on those
  - For example, a backend can document how an endpoint works, and another node can depend on that information.
    It then does not depend directly on the implementation, but specifically on the documented information in that node.

- Prompts and discussions are more important than the output result:
  - Verifications are second most important
  - With prompt graph and verifications we can regenerate implementations as much as we want:
    - For different runtimes
    - When new models come out
    - etc.



= Workflows =
== Initial feature ==
...
== Fixes ==
The user identifies some issue. Should be able to ask an agent which nodes are related to this issue.
Should get pointed to some initial description and associated implementations, and easily find where a new verification can be added.
Can do a few things:
  - Write a new automated verification, trigger the graph so it builds, runs, fails, and then let agent work the graph until other nodes are updated and implementation has been fixed (not touching the new verification node)
  - Add a node for manual verification, marked as failed. Trigger graph for new implementation, then when asked by the agent to do the manual verification, check again, and mark as complete if issue is fixed.
  - Add a new description node instructing an agent to add a verification node (and then run it). Run the graph, agents produce implementations of verification and runs until graph is all green again.




= Use git as the graph =
Git is fundamentally an acyclic graph, where each hash is a snapshot.
We could model the nodes of llaundry within the git graph and benefit from all the associated git tooling.


= Representation of Code/Implementation =
Note: This ideas is orthogonal to the core of llaundry, I think.
      The graph language can be an implementation category supported byh the build and verification tools in the llaundry MCP.

Instead of storing code as text, leverage a graph database.
Programming langauges are a connected graph of nodes carrying semantic meaning,
but also details of implementation, as well as abstractions to help humans work.
Instead, we could store pure intent in as a graph database, in a "definition graph", allowing for efficient
querying and editing by LLM agents.
For concrete implementation, the graph can be turned into code and built into artifacts, based on some set
of implementation characteristics (bare-metal, with or without some specific runtime etc.)

A concrete implementation can also be represented by a graph. LLM agents can work on the implementation graph
until a valid artifact can be produced, while not being allowed to edit the definition graph.

If the artifact is tested and exhibits problems, then the definition graph is edited, followed by the implementation graph
and the production of a new artifact.


= Implementation language =

Probably related project.
A language where the main constituents are:
  - Data types
    - Focus on what one wants to be able to do with the data, not the concrete representation
    - Example: "List of users, fast reads"
  - Modules and interfaces between them
  - Mutable state holders (inspired by OTP)
